question: Based on this image, are the findings normal or abnormal?
ground_truth_reasoning:
First, I examine the three intraoperative images (panels a–c) for visible anatomy and any abnormal structures. I note the labeled orientation (R, L, H, T) and that the surgical field exposes the spinal canal/dural region with several nerve rootlets visible. In each panel an arrow highlights a focal pale/whitish nodular tissue or opacity that is not part of the smooth, continuous nerve roots or the normally pale, smooth dural surface.

Next, I relate those observations to the question, which asks whether the findings are normal or abnormal. In panel a the arrowed structure is a small, discrete, pale mass being manipulated by instruments and stands out from the surrounding parallel nerve root fibers. In panel b the arrowed area shows local displacement/indentation of adjacent rootlets where the mass abuts them, suggesting local compression or adherence. In panel c the arrow points to an area where the dural surface appears interrupted or overlain by the same pale tissue and the normal smooth contours are altered.

Then I consider how these visual features line up with a normal intraoperative appearance: a normal exposed dura/nerve root field would show smooth dural surface and uniformly spaced, intact root fibers without focal nodular masses or localized displacement. The presence of a discrete nodular lesion, focal distortion of rootlets, and altered dural surface are direct visual signs of pathology in these images.

Therefore, based on the clearly visible focal lesion and its effect on adjacent neural structures, the images show abnormal findings.
caption: Intraoperative microscopic view.(a) Upon dural incision, white, spongy fragments (arrow) were immediately observed in the subarachnoid space. (b) The left L4 nerve root (arrow) was flattened because of extreme compression from the ventral side. (c) The lesion was found to originate from the inner left wall of the dura (arrow), but no penetrating hole was apparent.
sub_caption: (a) Upon dural incision, white, spongy fragments (arrow) were immediately observed in the subarachnoid space.
image_context: When the dura was incised, white debris was observed. This led us to assume a herniated disc (Figure <xref rid="cureus-0014-00000022418-i04" ref-type="fig">4a</xref>). The lesion was causing considerable compression of the posterior root of the L4 from the ventral side (Figure ). The lesion was causing considerable compression of the posterior root of the L4 from the ventral side (Figure <xref rid="cureus-0014-00000022418-i04" ref-type="fig">4b</xref>). The lesion originated from the left medial surface of the dura (Figure ). The lesion originated from the left medial surface of the dura (Figure <xref rid="cureus-0014-00000022418-i04" ref-type="fig">4c</xref>). The lesion's membrane had failed, and the contents were leaking out; therefore, the lesion was reduced in size by enucleation and was removed. No hole was found in the dura (Figure ). The lesion's membrane had failed, and the contents were leaking out; therefore, the lesion was reduced in size by enucleation and was removed. No hole was found in the dura (Figure <xref rid="cureus-0014-00000022418-i04" ref-type="fig">4c</xref>).).
